stages.title: Hallway dash
stages.prompt: Cross the hallway, collect the greeting, name, country, and question-back pieces, and skip the extra-detail decoys.
stages.correctMessage: Good. You crossed cleanly and picked up the full intro line in order.
stages.retryMessage: Run it again and collect the intro pieces in the order a real first meeting needs.

presentation.helperText: Build the intro rail in order as you cross the hallway. Skip details that come too early.
presentation.resolvedNote: You built a first-meeting opener in the order a real introduction needs.

stages.title: Reply rush
stages.prompt: Clear the best first-meeting question before the hallway clock runs out.

rounds.prompt: A new classmate says: Hi, I'm Ana.
rounds.dialoguePrompt: Pick the simple question that naturally comes next.

options.label: Where are you from?
options.detail: Best first follow-up after only the name.

options.label: What class are you in?
options.detail: Possible at school, but it skips past the simplest personal follow-up.

options.label: How old are you?
options.detail: Too personal for the first question in a new exchange.

rounds.prompt: You say: Hi, I'm Leo. I'm from Mexico.

options.label: What about you?
options.detail: Short, natural, and easy to answer.

options.label: And you?
options.detail: Close, but a little thinner than the strongest question-back.

options.label: What is your phone number?
options.detail: Too personal for the first exchange, even if the tone sounds friendly.

stages.correctMessage: Good. Your reply choices kept the first meeting sounding natural.
stages.retryMessage: Clear the quickest, most natural replies instead of jumping too far ahead.

presentation.helperText: Choose the reply that keeps the exchange light and easy for the other person to answer.
presentation.resolvedNote: You kept the exchange open without making it too personal too fast.

stages.title: Intro burst
stages.prompt: Say one short first-meeting line with your name, country, and one question back.
stages.targetPhrase: Hi, I'm Ana from Brazil. What about you?
stages.coachFocus: Keep the line short, bright, and easy for the other person to answer.
acceptedResponses: Hi, I'm Ana from Brazil. What about you?
Hello, I'm Ana from Brazil. What about you?
Hi, my name is Ana. I'm from Brazil. What about you?

fallbackOptions.label: Hi, I'm Ana from Brazil. What about you?
Hi, I'm Ana. I'm from Brazil.
Hello, I'm Ana from Brazil.

stages.correctMessage: Good. That burst line sounds ready for the speaking conversation.
stages.retryMessage: Keep the name, country, and question back together in one short burst.

presentation.helperText: Say it once or use the quick backup. This is the same line you want ready for speaking.
presentation.resolvedNote: You kept the intro short enough to sound like a real first meeting, not a speech.

stages.title: People sort
stages.prompt: Drop each person card into the lane that fits the best simple description.

cards.label: My brother likes soccer after school.
My friend is funny and easy to talk to.
My teacher is kind and helpful in class.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The people details are sorted cleanly.
stages.retryMessage: Sort each person detail into the strongest lane so the scene stays easy to follow.

presentation.helperText: Sort the people details fast so the family, friend, and teacher cards stop colliding.
presentation.resolvedNote: You separated the people details clearly enough to use them in a real conversation.

stages.title: Request pick
stages.prompt: Clear the polite classroom request before the queue backs up.

rounds.prompt: You need a pencil in class.
rounds.dialoguePrompt: Choose the line that sounds polite and specific, not demanding.

options.label: Excuse me, can I borrow your pencil for a minute?
Can I use your pencil after class?
options.detail: Polite, but it misses the right class-time moment

options.label: Do you have an extra pencil today?
options.detail: Possible, but less clear about borrowing

rounds.prompt: Now make the request sound short enough for class.

options.label: for a minute?
for this class?
options.detail: Possible, but longer than the clean short borrow request

options.label: until the end of class?
options.detail: Related, but less light than the quickest classroom ask

stages.correctMessage: Good. The classroom request now sounds polite and usable.
stages.retryMessage: Pick the request pieces that sound like real classroom English, not direct or vague language.

presentation.helperText: Clear the request choices that sound polite enough to use with a classmate or teacher.
presentation.resolvedNote: You kept the request polite, short, and realistic for class.

stages.title: Scene split
stages.prompt: Sort the final cards into Describe or Ask so the mini-scene feels complete.

cards.label: My teacher is kind and helpful.
My friend likes music after class.
Can I borrow your notebook for a minute?

stages.correctMessage: Good. You can now separate people details from classroom language without mixing the two.
stages.retryMessage: Sort the final scene so descriptions stay separate from classroom requests.

presentation.helperText: Finish the mini-scene by sorting description language away from classroom request language.
presentation.resolvedNote: You kept the people descriptions and classroom requests from blending together awkwardly.

stages.title: Morning to evening
stages.prompt: Run the clean route from wake-up to after-school work without taking the wrong branch.
stages.correctMessage: Good. The timeline route now sounds like one real day.
stages.retryMessage: Race the routine in order from morning to after-school time instead of skipping around.

presentation.helperText: Hit the time gates in order so the schedule feels smooth from morning to evening.
presentation.resolvedNote: You kept the schedule chronological instead of turning it into a random list.

stages.title: After-school branch
stages.prompt: Take the clean after-school branch and avoid the detour that breaks the routine.
stages.correctMessage: Good. You chose the branch that sounds like a believable weekday.
stages.retryMessage: Take the branch that keeps the after-school routine clear and believable.

presentation.helperText: Pick the route that still sounds like a normal school day, not a random detour.
presentation.resolvedNote: You kept the routine believable by choosing the branch that supports the day.

stages.title: Best day line
stages.prompt: Clear the strongest routine line before the timer empties.

rounds.prompt: Which summary sounds easiest to say in one smooth line?

options.label: After school I do homework, then I relax at home.
After school I do homework, and then I go to practice before I get home.
options.detail: Possible, but it changes the route you just built

options.label: After school I usually go to the mall for a while.
options.detail: Related, but it breaks the weekday routine path

stages.correctMessage: Good. That final line sounds ready for speaking.
stages.retryMessage: Pick the routine line that sounds natural from start to finish, not like disconnected words.

presentation.helperText: Clear the line that sounds like something a learner could really say out loud.
presentation.resolvedNote: You finished with one connected line instead of a list of separate pieces.

units.title: Food, Shopping, and Likes and Dislikes

stages.title: Order rush
stages.prompt: Sort the moving order cards into the receipt lanes before the counter gets crowded.
stages.correctMessage: Good. The counter receipt is complete and ready to say.
stages.retryMessage: Sort the receipt in order so the counter line sounds complete and polite.

presentation.helperText: Sort the moving counter pieces into one clean order receipt.
presentation.resolvedNote: You kept the order in the sequence a real customer would use at the counter.

stages.title: Counter follow-up
stages.prompt: Clear the follow-up lines that keep the order moving.

rounds.prompt: You already said: I'd like a sandwich and water, please.
rounds.dialoguePrompt: Which line is the strongest next move right after the order?

options.label: How much is it?
Can I pay by card?
Can I get it without cheese?
options.detail: Possible, but it changes the order instead of moving it forward

rounds.prompt: Now keep the exchange quick and polite.

options.label: Thanks.
That's everything, thanks.
options.detail: Close, but a little longer than the cleanest finish

options.label: Can I also get a fork?
options.detail: Possible, but it adds a new request instead of closing the exchange

stages.correctMessage: Good. Those follow-ups fit a real snack exchange.
stages.retryMessage: Clear the lines that keep the counter moving instead of changing the scene.

presentation.helperText: Pick the next lines a customer would really use right after ordering.
presentation.resolvedNote: You kept the exchange moving instead of piling on extra changes and side details.

stages.title: Order burst
stages.prompt: Say the final order once, clearly and politely.
stages.targetPhrase: I'd like a sandwich and water, please.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the food, drink, and polite finish in one short burst.
acceptedResponses: I'd like a sandwich and water, please.
Can I get a sandwich and water, please?

fallbackOptions.label: I'd like a sandwich and water, please.
Can I get a sandwich and water, please?
I'd like a sandwich and a water, please.

stages.correctMessage: Good. That order line is ready for the snack conversation.
stages.retryMessage: Keep the order short and steady so the cashier can answer quickly.

presentation.helperText: Say it once or use the quick backup. Keep the food, drink, and polite ending together.
presentation.resolvedNote: You kept the order smooth enough to sound like a real counter exchange.

stages.title: Street race
stages.prompt: Trace the clean walk from the school gate to the library before the timer drains.
stages.correctMessage: Good. That route is short, direct, and easy to explain.
stages.retryMessage: Race the path that gets to the library cleanly instead of drifting into the longer street detours.

presentation.helperText: Trace the quickest walk first. Wrong turns should feel like real detours, not random moves.
presentation.resolvedNote: You stayed on the shortest, easiest route instead of wandering into side streets.

stages.title: Landmark branch
stages.prompt: Choose the landmark branch that keeps the directions easy after the gate.
stages.correctMessage: Good. You picked the landmark branch that makes the route easy to explain.
stages.retryMessage: Take the branch with the strongest landmark instead of the longer, messier detour.

presentation.helperText: Pick the branch with the landmark you would actually say out loud in simple directions.
presentation.resolvedNote: You chose the landmark that helps the listener orient quickly instead of memorizing too much.

stages.title: Direction finish
stages.prompt: Clear the direction line that sounds easiest to follow.

rounds.prompt: A classmate asks: Can you help me find the library?

options.label: Go through the gate, then walk past the office to the crosswalk.
Walk past the garden and then head toward the library.
options.detail: Possible, but it sends the listener onto the longer branch.

options.label: Go by the parking lot and cut across later.
options.detail: Too vague, and it points to the dead-end side instead of the clean route.

stages.correctMessage: Good. That direction line sounds clean and usable.
stages.retryMessage: Pick the line that moves the listener one step at a time instead of overloading them.

presentation.helperText: Hit the line that sounds like real simple directions, not a long confusing explanation.
presentation.resolvedNote: You finished with a line that a classmate could actually follow while walking.

stages.title: Weekend plan pick
stages.prompt: Lock the clear weekend plan before the forecast starts shifting.

rounds.prompt: Your friend asks: What are you going to do this weekend?

options.label: I'm going to visit my friend on Saturday.
I want good weather for the park this weekend.
options.detail: Preference, but not the actual plan

options.label: My friend is free on Saturday afternoon.
options.detail: Useful detail, but still not the plan itself

stages.correctMessage: Good. The plan is clear before the weather changes.
stages.retryMessage: Choose the line that actually states the plan before you try to adapt it.

presentation.helperText: Start with one real plan first. The weather shift only works if the plan is clear.
presentation.resolvedNote: You started with a real plan instead of a preference or side detail.

stages.title: Forecast flip
stages.prompt: React to each weather change before the timer drops and the plan stops making sense.

rounds.prompt: Sunny afternoon

options.label: Go to the park
Stay home and watch a movie
options.detail: Possible, but stronger as a rainy backup

options.label: Meet at a cafe for a while
options.detail: Works, but less natural than using the good weather

rounds.prompt: Now it starts raining.

options.label: Stay home and watch a movie
Meet at a cafe instead
options.detail: Possible backup, but weaker for the original plan

options.label: Still go to the park

rounds.prompt: The rain stops, but the evening turns cool.

options.label: Meet at a cafe
Go back to the park
options.detail: Possible, but less smooth after the forecast change

options.label: Stay home and watch a movie
options.detail: Works, but gives up too much after the rain stops

stages.correctMessage: Good. You adapted the plan fast enough to keep the weekend realistic.
stages.retryMessage: React to the forecast change with the strongest backup instead of vague or weak switches.

presentation.helperText: The forecast changes live. Hit the backup that keeps the weekend plan believable.
presentation.resolvedNote: You changed the plan when the weather actually demanded it instead of pretending nothing changed.

stages.title: Backup line
stages.prompt: Finish with the one backup sentence that sounds ready to say aloud.

rounds.prompt: If it rains, what should you say next?

options.label: If it rains, we can stay home and watch a movie instead.
If it rains, we can meet at a cafe instead.
options.detail: Possible, but less direct than the strongest backup

options.label: If it rains, we can decide later.
options.detail: Too open to feel like a real backup

stages.correctMessage: Good. That backup line sounds practical and ready to use.
stages.retryMessage: Choose the backup line that actually solves the weather problem.

presentation.helperText: Clear the sentence that sounds like a real plan change, not an unfinished idea.
presentation.resolvedNote: You finished with one usable backup sentence instead of leaving the plan open.

units.title: Habits and Routines in More Detail

stages.title: Weekend run

rounds.prompt: Panel 2 just ended. Which panel snaps in next and keeps the weekend moving?

options.label: We talked there for a while.
options.detail: Possible, but it is a softer side detail than the main next beat.

stages.retryMessage: Choose the beat that keeps the story moving forward.

stages.title: Story burst

stages.title: Queue rush

stages.title: Set the story order

presentation.helperText: Order the event, the tension, and the resolution before you tell the full story.

stages.title: Add the key details
stages.prompt: Fill the story slots so the listener gets the setting, the turning point, and the reflection.

options.label: during a museum trip
a staff member helped me find the entrance
it taught me to stay calm when plans change
I usually eat breakfast early
options.detail: Does not belong in this story

stages.correctMessage: Good. The story frame now includes the detail and the reflection that make it memorable.
stages.retryMessage: Fill the story frame with the setting, the turning point, and the reflection that belongs to this experience.

presentation.helperText: Build the frame so the story has context, movement, and a meaningful ending.

stages.title: Say the story frame
stages.prompt: Say a short version of the story that includes the event, the resolution, and what you learned.
stages.targetPhrase: One time, I got separated from my group during a museum trip, but in the end I found them again and learned to stay calm.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the main event, the ending, and the reflection in one smooth retell.
acceptedResponses: One time, I got separated from my group during a museum trip, but in the end I found them again and learned to stay calm.
One time, I got separated from my group on a museum trip, and in the end I found them again and learned to stay calm.

fallbackOptions.label: One time, I got separated from my group during a museum trip, but in the end I found them again and learned to stay calm.
One time, I got separated from my group during a museum trip.
I learned to stay calm in the end.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to tell the full story in speaking.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to a short retell that includes the event, the ending, and what you learned.

presentation.helperText: Say one compact version of the story before you expand it in speaking.

stages.title: Build the opinion stack
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so your opinion answer sounds clear and supported from the beginning.

options.label: I think part-time jobs can help students if the schedule stays reasonable.
One reason is that they can teach responsibility and time management.
For example, a small weekend job can help a student learn how to manage time.
Students take the bus home after class.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The answer now has a clear claim, support, and example.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the stack so the opinion comes first and the support stays connected to it.

presentation.helperText: Start with the opinion, then add a reason and an example that actually support it.

stages.title: Pick the strongest concession
stages.prompt: Which sentence acknowledges the other side without losing your main point?

options.label: On the other hand, too many work hours could hurt school performance, so balance matters.
options.detail: Acknowledges a drawback while keeping the opinion clear

options.label: Jobs exist in many cities and neighborhoods.
options.detail: Too vague to help the argument

options.label: I went to work with my cousin last summer.

stages.correctMessage: Good. That keeps the answer balanced without giving away the point.
stages.retryMessage: Choose the sentence that adds a real concession or drawback, not just another fact.

presentation.helperText: Pick the line that shows balance while still protecting the opinion.

stages.title: Say your opinion line
stages.prompt: Say a short opinion line that includes your position and one reason.
stages.targetPhrase: I think part-time jobs can help students if the schedule stays reasonable because they teach responsibility.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the opinion and the reason in the same line so the answer sounds stable from the start.
acceptedResponses: I think part-time jobs can help students if the schedule stays reasonable because they teach responsibility.
I think part-time jobs can help students if the schedule is reasonable because they teach responsibility.

fallbackOptions.label: I think part-time jobs can help students if the schedule stays reasonable because they teach responsibility.
I think part-time jobs can help students.
Responsibility is important for students.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to defend the opinion in speaking.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to one sentence that includes the opinion and one reason.

presentation.helperText: Say the opinion and the reason together so the discussion opens with a strong main point.

stages.title: Sort the fundraising ideas
stages.prompt: Place each idea where it belongs: best fit, possible backup, or weak option.

cards.label: Weekend car wash
cards.detail: Many students can join and it can raise money quickly.

cards.label: Lunch-time bake sale
cards.detail: Possible, but fewer people can prepare food every day.

cards.label: Sell expensive class hoodies
cards.detail: High cost and hard to organize fast.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The board now shows the strongest option and the weaker alternatives clearly.
stages.retryMessage: Sort the ideas so the best recommendation stands out from the backup and the weak option.

presentation.helperText: Sort the ideas by strength before you try to defend one recommendation.

stages.title: Build the recommendation
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so your decision line includes the recommendation, the reason, and the tradeoff.

options.label: A weekend car wash is the best option for the class trip.
More students can join, so it can raise money faster.
It depends on the weather, so we should also plan a backup date too.
Everyone likes warm weather in spring.

stages.correctMessage: Good. That recommendation sounds realistic and justified.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the recommendation so it includes the best option, the reason, and one practical tradeoff.

presentation.helperText: A strong benchmark answer should choose clearly and still show one real tradeoff.

stages.title: Say the decision pitch
stages.prompt: Say a short decision pitch that recommends one option and explains why it is the best fit.
stages.targetPhrase: I think a weekend car wash is the best option because more students can join and it can raise money quickly.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the recommendation and the reason together so the group hears the decision clearly.
acceptedResponses: I think a weekend car wash is the best option because more students can join and it can raise money quickly.
A weekend car wash is the best option because more students can join and it can raise money quickly.

fallbackOptions.label: I think a weekend car wash is the best option because more students can join and it can raise money quickly.
A weekend car wash is the best option.
More students can join and it can raise money quickly.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to defend the decision in the benchmark conversation.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to one sentence that gives the best option and the reason together.

presentation.helperText: Say the recommendation in one smooth line before the benchmark follow-up pressure starts.

stages.title: Find the support signal
stages.prompt: Tap the two source details that actually support the main idea about different class formats.

hotspots.label: Flexibility
hotspots.detail: Online classes help some students manage time

hotspots.label: Discussion
hotspots.detail: In-person classes can support stronger classroom interaction

hotspots.label: Lunch menu
hotspots.detail: This does not support the reading summary

stages.correctMessage: Good. Those details actually support the main idea.
stages.retryMessage: Tap the details that belong in a summary of the article, not random information around it.

presentation.helperText: Pick the details that would belong in a good short summary.

stages.title: Build the summary-response line
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so the answer includes the main idea, one supporting detail, and your response.

options.label: Different class formats support different kinds of learners.
For example, online classes offer flexibility while in-person classes can improve discussion and focus.
I agree that a mixed approach can work well because students do not all learn in the same way.
The school bus arrives very early every day.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The response now sounds like a real summary plus response, not just a copied fact.
stages.retryMessage: Build the line with the main idea, one supporting detail, and a response in your own words.

presentation.helperText: A strong answer should summarize first, then react in your own words.

stages.title: Pick the strongest paraphrase
stages.prompt: Which sentence sounds most like a clear paraphrase instead of a copied note?

options.label: The article suggests that no single class format works best for every learner.
The article says different formats support different learners, flexibility, and discussion.
School is different from class and homework.

stages.correctMessage: Good. That sounds like a learner summary, not a copied line.
stages.retryMessage: Choose the line that sounds like a natural paraphrase of the main idea.

presentation.helperText: Pick the version that sounds closest to how you would explain the article in your own words.

stages.title: Map the next steps
stages.prompt: Place each step where it belongs in the path: now, next, or later.

cards.label: Read one English article every day
Apply for the debate team next year

stages.correctMessage: Good. The path now moves from the first step to the longer goal clearly.
stages.retryMessage: Sort the path so the immediate step, the next routine, and the long-term goal make sense together.

presentation.helperText: Build the path from the first practical step to the bigger future goal.

stages.title: Build the goal line
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so the answer includes the goal, the first step, and one challenge or possibility.

options.label: My goal is to improve my English enough to join the debate team next year.
First, I'm going to practice speaking every week and read more in English.
If possible, I'd also like to take an extra writing class, but time may be the main challenge.
I usually take the bus after school.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The plan now sounds realistic and connected.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the line so it names the goal, the first step, and one likely challenge or possibility.

presentation.helperText: A strong future answer should sound concrete, not just hopeful.

stages.title: Say the goal line
stages.prompt: Say a short future-plan line that includes the goal and the first step.
stages.targetPhrase: My goal is to improve my English enough to join the debate team next year, and first I'm going to practice speaking every week.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the goal and the first step together so the plan sounds concrete from the beginning.
acceptedResponses: My goal is to improve my English enough to join the debate team next year, and first I'm going to practice speaking every week.
My goal is to improve my English enough to join the debate team next year, and first I am going to practice speaking every week.

fallbackOptions.label: My goal is to improve my English enough to join the debate team next year, and first I'm going to practice speaking every week.
My goal is to improve my English next year.
I'm going to practice speaking every week.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to explain the full plan in speaking.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to one sentence that includes both the goal and the first step.

presentation.helperText: Say the goal and the first step together before you expand the plan in speaking.

stages.title: Build the intro board
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so the opening includes your interest, your strength, and one concrete example.

slots.label: Example
slots.detail: Proof from school, work, or community

options.label: I'm interested in this role because I enjoy helping people and working with a team.
One strength I have is that I stay calm under pressure.
For example, I helped organize a student event last semester and made sure everyone knew what to do.
I sometimes take the bus downtown after class.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The opening now sounds like a credible interview answer.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the intro so it shows interest, a real strength, and a concrete example.

presentation.helperText: A strong interview answer should sound motivated, specific, and supported by one example.

stages.title: Pick the strongest follow-up answer
stages.prompt: Which answer best responds to a follow-up question about handling new situations?

options.label: When I face a new situation, I ask clear questions, learn the routine quickly, and stay calm while I adjust.
I like trying new things and meeting people.
My classes are busy this semester.

stages.correctMessage: Good. That answer sounds calm, direct, and professional.
stages.retryMessage: Choose the answer that responds to the follow-up directly instead of repeating the opening.

presentation.helperText: Pick the reply that handles a real interview follow-up with connected detail.

stages.title: Say the interview opener
stages.prompt: Say a short interview opener that includes your interest, one strength, and one example.
stages.targetPhrase: I'm interested in this role because I enjoy helping people and working with a team. One strength I have is that I stay calm under pressure. For example, I helped organize a student event last semester.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the interest, the strength, and the example connected so the answer sounds prepared instead of memorized.
acceptedResponses: I'm interested in this role because I enjoy helping people and working with a team. One strength I have is that I stay calm under pressure. For example, I helped organize a student event last semester.

I am interested in this role because I enjoy helping people and working with a team. One strength I have is that I stay calm under pressure. For example, I helped organize a student event last semester.

fallbackOptions.label: I'm interested in this role because I enjoy helping people and working with a team. One strength I have is that I stay calm under pressure. For example, I helped organize a student event last semester.
I'm interested in this role because I like helping people.
One strength I have is that I stay calm under pressure.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to open the benchmark interview with a clear and supported answer.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to one answer that includes the interest, the strength, and the example together.

presentation.helperText: Say the opening once so the benchmark conversation starts with a clear, supported introduction.

stages.title: Find the real support
stages.prompt: Tap the two details that actually support the article's main claim about free transportation for students.

hotspots.label: Access barrier
hotspots.detail: Transportation costs can limit access to school and activities.

hotspots.label: Attendance support
hotspots.detail: Lower travel cost could help students attend more regularly.

hotspots.label: Lunch detail
hotspots.detail: This point does not support the transportation claim.

stages.correctMessage: Good. Those details actually support the claim.
stages.retryMessage: Choose the details that function as evidence for the argument, not unrelated facts.

presentation.helperText: Find the details that actually strengthen the argument before you evaluate it.

stages.title: Build the evaluation
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so your evaluation names the claim, rates the evidence, and adds one weakness.

slots.label: Evaluation
slots.detail: Judge how convincing the evidence is

slots.label: Weakness
slots.detail: Add one real limitation or assumption

options.label: The main claim is that students should have free public transportation.
The evidence is partly convincing because it shows access barriers clearly.
One weakness is that the article does not explain who would pay for the program.
Students often enjoy traveling with friends.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The evaluation now sounds analytical instead of descriptive.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the line so it identifies the claim, judges the evidence, and names one real weakness.

presentation.helperText: A strong advanced answer should evaluate the evidence, not only repeat the article.

stages.title: Say the evaluation line
stages.prompt: Say one short evaluation that names the claim and gives one limitation.
stages.targetPhrase: The main claim is that students should have free public transportation, but the evidence is only partly convincing because the article never explains who would pay for it.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the claim and the limitation together so the evaluation sounds controlled and precise.
acceptedResponses: The main claim is that students should have free public transportation, but the evidence is only partly convincing because the article never explains who would pay for it.
The main claim is that students should have free public transportation, but the evidence is partly convincing because the article never explains who would pay for it.

fallbackOptions.label: The main claim is that students should have free public transportation, but the evidence is only partly convincing because the article never explains who would pay for it.
The main claim is that students should have free public transportation.
The evidence is partly convincing.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to evaluate the argument in speaking.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to one line that names the claim and the limitation together.

presentation.helperText: Say one compact critique before you expand the analysis in speaking.

units.title: Speak and Write in Formal Registers

stages.title: Build the formal version
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so the sentence sounds more formal, precise, and audience-aware.

options.label: In my view,
students require adequate rest in order to perform effectively in school
therefore, later start times may better support learning.
kids need more sleep, you know

stages.correctMessage: Good. The sentence now sounds formal and controlled.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the line so the tone and phrasing fit a teacher, report, or formal audience.

presentation.helperText: Move the idea from casual wording into a cleaner formal register.

stages.title: Choose the strongest justification
stages.prompt: Which explanation best shows why the formal version works better?

options.label: It sounds more precise, avoids casual wording, and fits a teacher or employer audience.
It is longer, so it must be better.
options.detail: Length is not the real reason

options.label: It uses a difficult word, which is enough.
options.detail: Vocabulary alone is not register control

stages.correctMessage: Good. That explanation focuses on audience and precision.
stages.retryMessage: Choose the reason that explains the register shift, not just the sentence length.

presentation.helperText: Pick the explanation that actually shows why the revised sentence is more formal.

stages.title: Say the formal line
stages.prompt: Say the formal version clearly in one controlled sentence.
stages.targetPhrase: In my view, students require adequate rest in order to perform effectively in school, and therefore later start times may better support learning.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the formal phrasing smooth so it sounds usable in a real presentation or response.
acceptedResponses: In my view, students require adequate rest in order to perform effectively in school, and therefore later start times may better support learning.
In my view, students require adequate rest in order to perform effectively in school, therefore later start times may better support learning.

fallbackOptions.label: In my view, students require adequate rest in order to perform effectively in school, and therefore later start times may better support learning.
In my view, students require adequate rest in school.
Later start times may support learning.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to use the formal line in speaking.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to one formal sentence that keeps the precise wording and the connector.

presentation.helperText: Say the revised line once so the formal response feels natural before speaking.

stages.title: Sort the debate pieces
stages.prompt: Place each card where it belongs: main position, likely objection, or weak support.

cards.label: Homework should be reduced in lower grades.
Some people worry that less homework could reduce academic rigor.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The board now separates the real stance, the likely objection, and the weak support.
stages.retryMessage: Sort the debate cards so the argument and the objection are clear before you build the rebuttal.

presentation.helperText: A strong benchmark answer needs a clear stance and a clear objection to answer.

stages.title: Build the rebuttal
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so the rebuttal acknowledges the objection and answers it with a stronger reason.

options.label: I still believe homework should be reduced in lower grades.
It is true that schools need to protect academic standards,
but quality practice in class can matter more than a large amount of homework after school.
Students usually take the bus home in the afternoon.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The rebuttal now sounds balanced and persuasive.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the line so it keeps the stance, acknowledges the objection, and answers it directly.

presentation.helperText: A benchmark debate answer should concede briefly and then return to the stronger point.

stages.title: Say the rebuttal
stages.prompt: Say one short rebuttal that keeps the stance and answers the objection.
stages.targetPhrase: I still believe homework should be reduced in lower grades because quality practice in class can matter more than a large amount of homework after school.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the stance and the reason together so the benchmark response stays steady under pressure.
acceptedResponses: I still believe homework should be reduced in lower grades because quality practice in class can matter more than a large amount of homework after school.
I believe homework should be reduced in lower grades because quality practice in class can matter more than a large amount of homework after school.

fallbackOptions.label: I still believe homework should be reduced in lower grades because quality practice in class can matter more than a large amount of homework after school.
I still believe homework should be reduced in lower grades.
Quality practice in class can matter more.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to defend the position in the benchmark debate.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to one sentence that keeps the stance and the rebuttal reason together.

presentation.helperText: Say the rebuttal once so the benchmark debate opens with a controlled position.

units.title: Interpret Complex Texts and Implied Meaning

stages.title: Find the inference clues
stages.prompt: Tap the two details that best support the idea that the main character has changed by the end of the story.

hotspots.label: Final image
hotspots.detail: The ending image feels calm instead of resistant.

hotspots.label: Bus schedule
hotspots.detail: This detail does not support the inference.

stages.correctMessage: Good. Those clues actually support the inference.
stages.retryMessage: Pick the details that help explain the implied meaning, not just any detail from the text.

presentation.helperText: Find the details that let you justify an interpretation instead of guessing.

stages.title: Build the interpretation
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so the answer states the inference, the support, and the meaning of the tone.

options.label: The ending implies that the main character has changed.
The final image suggests a new sense of acceptance instead of resistance.
The quieter tone supports that interpretation by making the ending feel calmer.
The story takes place in a city with buses.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The interpretation now has an inference, evidence, and tone support.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the line so it explains the implied meaning instead of only repeating the plot.

presentation.helperText: A strong advanced answer should connect implied meaning to a specific clue and tone effect.

stages.title: Pick the stronger alternative reading
stages.prompt: Which alternative reading sounds plausible enough to mention without replacing your main interpretation?

options.label: Another possible reading is that the character is still uncertain, but the calmer ending tone makes acceptance more convincing.
The text is only about the weather and nothing else.
There are no other possible meanings in literature.

stages.correctMessage: Good. That keeps the interpretation open without losing the main reading.
stages.retryMessage: Choose the alternative reading that sounds plausible and still respects the evidence.

presentation.helperText: Advanced interpretation allows another meaning, but it still needs evidence and tone support.

stages.title: Sort the proposal pieces
stages.prompt: Place each card where it belongs: objective, benefit, or clarification detail.

cards.label: Move the orientation session online before the first week.
Students can get the information earlier and arrive more prepared.
The school could still offer in-person help for students who need it.
cards.detail: Clarifies how the proposal would work.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The proposal now has a clear objective, benefit, and clarification.
stages.retryMessage: Sort the cards so the recommendation sounds organized and presentation-ready.

presentation.helperText: A professional recommendation should explain the objective, the benefit, and one clarifying detail.

stages.title: Build the recommendation line
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so the answer gives the recommendation, the purpose, and the benefit in one controlled frame.

options.label: I recommend moving the orientation session online before the first week of class.
The main objective is to give students access to the information earlier.
This approach would reduce confusion and help students arrive more prepared.
Students often prefer afternoon events in spring.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The recommendation now sounds organized and professional.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the line so it states the proposal, the objective, and the benefit clearly.

presentation.helperText: A strong recommendation should make the proposal, purpose, and value obvious right away.

stages.title: Say the recommendation
stages.prompt: Say one short professional recommendation that includes the proposal and the main objective.
stages.targetPhrase: I recommend moving the orientation session online before the first week of class because the main objective is to give students access to the information earlier.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the proposal and the objective in the same line so the recommendation sounds structured and direct.
acceptedResponses: I recommend moving the orientation session online before the first week of class because the main objective is to give students access to the information earlier.
I recommend moving the orientation session online before the first week of class. The main objective is to give students access to the information earlier.

fallbackOptions.label: I recommend moving the orientation session online before the first week of class because the main objective is to give students access to the information earlier.
I recommend moving the orientation session online before the first week of class.
The main objective is to give students access to the information earlier.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to present the recommendation in speaking.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to one structured line that combines the proposal and the objective.

presentation.helperText: Say the recommendation once so the professional explanation starts with a clean structure.

stages.title: Sort the capstone inputs
stages.prompt: Place each card where it belongs: strongest evidence, key tradeoff, or practical next step.

cards.label: More sleep may improve concentration and attention in class.
Transportation schedules would need adjustment if the day starts later.
Test a 30-minute later start as a pilot program first.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The capstone board now separates the evidence, the tradeoff, and the recommendation path clearly.
stages.retryMessage: Sort the cards so the final answer can synthesize evidence and tradeoffs instead of sounding one-sided.

presentation.helperText: A capstone recommendation needs evidence, a tradeoff, and a practical next step.

stages.title: Build the capstone recommendation
stages.prompt: Fill the slots so the final answer includes the evidence, the tradeoff, and the practical recommendation.

slots.label: Tradeoff
slots.detail: What the committee must still weigh

options.label: The strongest reason is that students may focus better with more sleep.
However, transportation schedules would still need adjustment.
I recommend a 30-minute later start as a pilot program next year.
Students like later starts because mornings feel easier.

stages.correctMessage: Good. The capstone answer now sounds balanced and actionable.
stages.retryMessage: Rebuild the recommendation so it uses evidence, acknowledges a tradeoff, and ends with a practical next step.

presentation.helperText: A strong capstone answer should synthesize evidence and tradeoffs before recommending action.

stages.title: Say the capstone line
stages.prompt: Say one short recommendation that includes the strongest evidence and the practical next step.
stages.targetPhrase: Based on the evidence, I recommend a 30-minute later start as a pilot program because students may focus better with more sleep.
stages.coachFocus: Keep the evidence and the recommendation connected so the capstone answer sounds justified from the start.
acceptedResponses: Based on the evidence, I recommend a 30-minute later start as a pilot program because students may focus better with more sleep.
Based on the evidence, I recommend a 30 minute later start as a pilot program because students may focus better with more sleep.

fallbackOptions.label: Based on the evidence, I recommend a 30-minute later start as a pilot program because students may focus better with more sleep.
I recommend a 30-minute later start as a pilot program.
Students may focus better with more sleep.

stages.correctMessage: Good. You are ready to open the capstone benchmark with a balanced recommendation.
stages.retryMessage: Stay closer to one line that connects the evidence to the recommendation directly.

presentation.helperText: Say the evidence-based recommendation once so the benchmark answer opens with synthesis instead of scattered points.
